critic_raw:
The temporal split is appropriately designed (training months 1-36, validation 37-48, test 49-60) and patient-level integrity is enforced — both are methodological strengths. However, a significant preprocessing leakage issue is introduced in Section 3: 'Numerical features are standardized using dataset-wide statistics (mean and standard deviation)' for the structured data branch. Fitting standardization on the entire dataset before splitting allows test-set distribution information to influence the normalization applied during training, which is particularly problematic because the test window (months 49-60) may represent a different patient population from the training period.

The echocardiogram image availability is critical to this experiment's validity but is not adequately addressed. In real clinical settings, echo images are not available for all heart failure patients — some receive other imaging or no imaging. The design only notes this confound in a post-hoc subgroup analysis ('documentation completeness: performance for complete vs. partial imaging availability'). If the multi-modal model's test set contains a systematically different proportion of patients with echo images than the training set — plausible given patient selection shifts over time — the performance advantage may be driven by patient severity (sicker patients more likely to have echos) rather than imaging signal.

Both models use frozen pretrained encoders (BioBERT for text, DenseNet-121 for images) without any task-specific fine-tuning. This is a conservative design choice but introduces a strong prior: the image features extracted by DenseNet-121 (pretrained on ImageNet) may not capture clinically relevant cardiac structural information from echocardiograms. Frozen ImageNet features applied to medical echo images likely underperform compared to fine-tuned or medical-imaging-pretrained models, potentially biasing the experiment against finding an image benefit.

The hypothesis test requires that multi-modal gains are consistent 'across all subgroups.' This is an excessively strict criterion — almost no clinical model outperforms across every stratified subgroup simultaneously. If the multi-modal model shows higher overall AUROC but lower performance in one demographic stratum, the design's stated criterion for hypothesis support would reject a likely genuinely superior model.
all_issues_raised: Dataset-wide standardization of structured features leaks test-set distribution statistics into training normalization despite using temporal split
Echo image availability confound: patients with imaging may be systematically sicker, and image availability rates may shift across the temporal split windows
Frozen ImageNet-pretrained DenseNet-121 features likely insufficient for echocardiogram-specific cardiac structure — creates systematic bias against finding imaging benefit
Overly strict hypothesis criterion: requiring multi-modal superiority 'across all subgroups' will reject genuinely better models that show heterogeneous performance

Missing patient from 20,000 records: with 5-year EHR data and ~20,000 heart failure records, many patients will have multiple admissions, but patient-level temporal assignment is not explicitly verified beyond the stated criterion

Structured feature preprocessing leakage: numerical feature standardization uses dataset-wide statistics rather than training-set-only statistics, contaminating evaluation with test-set distributional information

Survivorship bias from mortality exclusion: excluding patients who died within 30 days creates a systematically healthier evaluation cohort that does not represent the full discharged population encountered in deployment

Domain mismatch in imaging pretrained model: DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet (natural images) is used frozen for echocardiogram encoding, where ultrasound-specific features (speckle noise, cardiac motion artifacts) bear no resemblance to ImageNet training distribution

Single-site limitation without external validation: the full 5-year dataset comes from one hospital system; no external validation cohort is specified, limiting generalizability claims for a model intended for clinical deployment

Lack of modality ablation: reporting only the full multimodal model performance without ablating individual modalities (text-only, imaging-only, structured-only) makes it impossible to determine whether the imaging or text modalities contribute positively or negatively to predictions
Structured data preprocessing (z-score normalization) fit on 'dataset-wide statistics' rather than training-set-only statistics, introducing preprocessing leakage for structured features
Baseline is a unimodal text-only model rather than a structured-data-only or clinical-standard-of-care model, limiting the experiment's ability to isolate the contribution of the imaging modality
AUROC chosen as primary metric over AUPRC for a class-imbalanced dataset (8-12% positive rate), where AUPRC provides more informative minority-class discrimination
Hyperparameter search covers only 20 random trials for the multi-modal model, which may be underpowered given the complexity of the joint model with three branches

No ablation study to isolate the contribution of each modality (text, imaging, structured data) — the experiment only compares full multi-modal vs. text-only, leaving the marginal contribution of imaging ambiguous
all_issues_adjudicated: Dataset-wide standardization of structured features leaks test-set distribution statistics into training normalization despite using temporal split
Echo image availability confound: patients with imaging may be systematically sicker, and image availability rates may shift across the temporal split windows
